title: Local Law 152 Is a Safety Requirement and a Liability Shield for Landlords
summary: Why this law matters for NYC owners, how noncompliance creates legal and financial exposure, and what records help protect landlords when liability is questioned.
content:
Why This Matters for Landlords

Gas-related incidents can create severe legal, financial, and reputational exposure. Local Law 152 requires periodic gas piping inspections so issues are found early and corrected before they become emergencies. Ignoring this process can leave landlords vulnerable to DOB enforcement, insurance complications, tenant claims, and avoidable repair costs.

Liability Risk Is Not Just About Fines

Many owners focus only on penalties, but liability extends much further. If an incident occurs, stakeholders will ask whether ownership followed required inspection and reporting obligations. A missing or delayed compliance record can make legal defense harder and increase settlement risk.

  • Documented inspections show proactive safety management.
  • Timely GPS-1 and GPS-2 filings demonstrate regulatory compliance.
  • Correction records support defensible operations when liability is questioned.
  • Consistent inspection cycles reduce long-term operational and financial risk.

What a Strong Compliance Program Looks Like

Effective Local Law 152 management is a repeatable process: schedule inspections based on your community district cycle, complete corrective work quickly, submit required filings on time, and maintain organized documentation across your portfolio.

Bottom Line

Local Law 152 is a safety obligation and a liability control strategy. For landlords, the cost of noncompliance often exceeds the cost of doing inspections correctly and on time.

title: What Happens During a Gas Piping Inspection?
summary: A field-level walkthrough of what licensed inspectors review, how findings are documented, and what owners should prepare in advance.
content:
Before the Inspector Arrives

A Local Law 152 gas piping inspection begins before the inspector walks through the door. Building owners or managers should ensure access to all gas-related areas: boiler rooms, mechanical spaces, risers, meter rooms, and individual units where gas appliances are connected. Locked areas and tenant no-shows are the most common reasons inspections get delayed or require return visits.

What Gets Inspected

A licensed master plumber (LMP) or a qualified individual working under an LMP’s supervision visually inspects all exposed gas piping from the point of entry to each connected appliance. The inspection covers:

  • Piping condition — looking for corrosion, damage, improper supports, or unauthorized modifications.
  • Connections and fittings — checking for leaks, loose joints, and deteriorated flexible connectors.
  • Shut-off valves — confirming they exist, are accessible, and can be operated.
  • Appliance connections — verifying proper hookups and that gas equipment is in safe operating condition.
  • Ventilation and clearances — ensuring gas equipment has adequate airflow and is not obstructed.

The inspector may use a combustible gas detector to test for active leaks at connections and fittings.

How Findings Are Documented

After the inspection, results fall into one of two categories:

  • No conditions found — the inspector files a GPS-1 certification with the NYC Department of Buildings, confirming the gas piping system passed.
  • Conditions found — the inspector documents each issue, files a GPS-1 noting the conditions, and the building owner must complete corrective work. Once repairs are done, a GPS-2 is filed to close out the conditions.

All findings are recorded with location details so corrective work can be assigned and tracked efficiently.

What Owners Should Know

The inspection itself is typically completed in a few hours for smaller buildings and may take a full day or multiple visits for larger properties. The biggest factor in timeline is access. Buildings that prepare in advance — coordinating tenant access, unlocking mechanical areas, and having prior inspection records available — move through the process faster and with fewer complications.

title: GPS-1 vs GPS-2: Key Filing Differences
summary: Understand which filing is required, when correction follow-up is needed, and how to avoid deadline issues with the NYC DOB.
content:
Two Filings, Two Purposes

Local Law 152 compliance involves two DOB filings: the GPS-1 and the GPS-2. They serve different purposes in the inspection and correction process, and confusing them is one of the most common mistakes building owners make.

GPS-1: The Inspection Certification

The GPS-1 is filed by the licensed master plumber after the gas piping inspection is completed. It certifies that the inspection took place and reports the results. A GPS-1 must be filed regardless of whether conditions were found.

  • Filed after every inspection, pass or fail.
  • Documents the scope of the inspection and any conditions identified.
  • Must be submitted to the DOB within the compliance deadline for your community district.
  • A late or missing GPS-1 can trigger violations and fines up to $10,000.

GPS-2: The Correction Certification

The GPS-2 is only required when the GPS-1 reports conditions that need corrective work. Once repairs are completed, the LMP files a GPS-2 to certify that all identified issues have been resolved.

  • Filed only when the GPS-1 identified conditions.
  • Must be submitted within 120 days of the GPS-1 filing (for non-hazardous conditions).
  • Hazardous conditions require immediate correction and notification to the utility company.
  • A missing GPS-2 leaves the compliance cycle incomplete, even if repairs were actually done.

Common Filing Mistakes

The most frequent issues we see with GPS filings:

  • Assuming a clean inspection means no filing is needed — the GPS-1 is always required.
  • Completing repairs but forgetting to file the GPS-2 to close out conditions.
  • Missing the 120-day GPS-2 deadline because corrective work was delayed.
  • Filing under the wrong BIN number in multi-building portfolios.

How to Stay on Track

Treat the GPS-1 and GPS-2 as a linked pair. When an inspection is scheduled, the filing timeline should already be mapped out. If conditions are found, corrective work and the GPS-2 deadline should be tracked together so nothing falls through the cracks.

title: How to Keep Your 4-Year Inspection Cycle on Track
summary: A simple scheduling framework for portfolios so inspections, repairs, and reporting are completed on time.
content:
The 4-Year Cycle Explained

Local Law 152 requires gas piping inspections on a rolling 4-year cycle, with deadlines staggered by community district. Each cycle resets after the previous inspection is filed with the DOB. Missing a cycle does not push the next one back — it creates a gap in compliance history that can trigger enforcement action.

Why Portfolio Owners Fall Behind

Single-building owners can usually manage one deadline. But for owners and managers with multiple properties across different community districts, the challenge compounds quickly:

  • Different buildings hit different deadlines in different years.
  • Tenant access coordination takes time, especially in occupied residential buildings.
  • Corrective work from one inspection can overlap with scheduling the next.
  • Staff turnover means institutional knowledge of compliance history gets lost.

A Simple Scheduling Framework

Staying compliant across a portfolio does not require complex software. It requires a repeatable process:

  1. Map your deadlines. List every building by BIN, community district, and next inspection due date. This is your master schedule.
  2. Start early. Begin scheduling inspections 6–9 months before the deadline. This builds in buffer time for tenant coordination, access issues, and repair work.
  3. Track corrections separately. If a GPS-1 flags conditions, the GPS-2 has its own 120-day deadline. Track it as a separate task, not an afterthought.
  4. Centralize records. Keep GPS-1 and GPS-2 filings, inspection reports, and repair receipts in one place per building. You will need them for audits, insurance, and future inspections.
  5. Review annually. At the start of each year, review which buildings are due in the next 12–18 months and confirm your inspection provider has capacity.

What Happens When You Fall Behind

A missed cycle is not just a fine. It creates a gap in your compliance record that is visible to the DOB, insurers, and potential buyers or lenders during due diligence. The longer the gap, the harder it is to explain. Staying ahead of the schedule is always less expensive than catching up after enforcement begins.

title: How CityWatch Helps Reduce Violation Risk
summary: Use real-time violation monitoring to detect new issues, assign action owners, and keep records organized.
content:
What Is CityWatch?

CityWatch is a free monitoring tool developed by Environmental Safeguard Solutions LLC that tracks Department of Buildings violations in real time across your building portfolio. It pulls data directly from NYC open data sources and alerts you when new violations are issued against any of your properties.

Why Violations Slip Through the Cracks

Most building owners discover violations late — sometimes during a sale, refinance, or insurance renewal. By that point, penalties may have escalated, cure periods may have lapsed, and the cost of resolution has increased. The root problem is visibility: if you are not actively checking DOB records for every building you manage, new violations can go unnoticed for months.

How CityWatch Works

  • Portfolio monitoring. Add your buildings by BIN or address. CityWatch monitors them continuously.
  • Real-time alerts. When a new violation is recorded against any of your properties, you are notified promptly.
  • Status tracking. See which violations are open, which are in cure, and which have been resolved — all in one view.
  • Action assignment. Assign violations to team members or contractors so nothing sits unaddressed.

How It Fits Into Compliance

CityWatch complements your Local Law 152 inspection program. While inspections are periodic, violations can be issued at any time — from tenant complaints, utility reports, or DOB sweeps. Monitoring both your inspection schedule and your violation history gives you a more complete picture of your compliance posture.

Getting Started

CityWatch is available at no cost to Gas Pro Inspectors clients. If you manage multiple buildings and want better visibility into your violation history, ask your account contact about setting up your portfolio in CityWatch.

title: How ESS Supports NYC Compliance Programs
summary: Learn how Environmental Safeguard Solutions LLC supports inspection, reporting, and compliance workflows across NYC assets.
content:
About Environmental Safeguard Solutions

Environmental Safeguard Solutions LLC (ESS) is the parent company behind Gas Pro Inspectors and CityWatch. ESS was founded to help NYC building owners manage regulatory compliance more efficiently — starting with Local Law 152 gas piping inspections and expanding into violation monitoring, filing support, and compliance program management.

What ESS Provides

ESS supports building owners, property managers, and management companies across all five boroughs with a full compliance workflow:

  • Gas piping inspections. Licensed master plumber-led inspections under the Gas Pro Inspectors brand, covering residential and commercial buildings of all sizes.
  • GPS-1 and GPS-2 filings. Inspections include preparation and submission of required DOB filings so owners do not need to manage paperwork separately.
  • Corrective work coordination. When conditions are found, ESS helps coordinate repairs through qualified contractors and tracks the GPS-2 filing deadline.
  • Violation monitoring. CityWatch provides real-time DOB violation tracking across building portfolios at no additional cost to clients.
  • Compliance record management. ESS maintains organized inspection histories, filing confirmations, and correction records for each building in your portfolio.

Who We Work With

ESS serves a range of NYC property stakeholders:

  • Individual building owners with one or two properties.
  • Property management companies overseeing dozens or hundreds of buildings.
  • Co-op and condo boards managing shared building systems.
  • Commercial landlords with retail, office, or mixed-use portfolios.

Our Approach

Compliance should not require owners to become regulatory experts. ESS handles the technical inspection work, the DOB filing process, and the follow-up tracking so that building owners can focus on operations. Every engagement is structured around clear timelines, transparent communication, and complete documentation.

title: Pre-Inspection Checklist for Property Teams
summary: A quick checklist of access, records, and communication items that help inspections run efficiently and avoid rework.
content:
Why Preparation Matters

The most common reason gas piping inspections take longer than expected is not the inspection itself — it is access. Locked mechanical rooms, unresponsive tenants, and missing keys account for the majority of delays and return visits. A small amount of preparation before inspection day saves time, reduces cost, and helps avoid incomplete inspections that require rescheduling.

Access Checklist

  • Confirm the inspector will have access to the boiler room, meter room, and all mechanical spaces.
  • Unlock or provide keys for riser closets on every floor.
  • Notify tenants at least 48 hours in advance that the inspector will need to enter apartments with gas appliances (stoves, dryers, in-unit heaters).
  • Designate a building staff member (super, porter, or manager) to accompany the inspector and facilitate access throughout the building.
  • For buildings with restricted-access areas (commercial tenants, locked basements), arrange entry in advance.

Records Checklist

  • Have the building’s BIN (Building Identification Number) confirmed and available.
  • Pull prior inspection reports if available — the inspector can reference previous findings.
  • If corrective work was done since the last inspection, have receipts or contractor documentation ready.
  • Know where the gas meter and main shut-off valve are located.

Communication Checklist

  • Confirm the inspection date and estimated arrival time with your inspection provider.
  • Share the building contact’s phone number with the inspector for day-of coordination.
  • If the building has a management company, confirm who is the point of contact for inspection-day issues.
  • For multi-building inspections, confirm the order of buildings and estimated time per building.

Day-Of Reminders

  • Ensure the building entrance and lobby are accessible (no lockouts).
  • Keep the designated staff member available for the full inspection window.
  • If a tenant is unavailable, document the attempt — the inspector may need to note inaccessible areas.
  • Ask the inspector about preliminary findings before they leave so you can begin planning any corrective work immediately.
